channel.title: NPR Topics: Home Page Top Stories
channel.description: Top stories in the U.S. and world news, politics, health, science, business, music, arts and culture. Nonprofit journalism with a mission. This is NPR.
channel.copyright: Copyright 2024 NPR - For Personal Use Only

image.title: NPR Topics: Home Page Top Stories

item.title: In Vermont, small town meetings grapple with debate on big issues
item.description: Typically concerned with local issues, residents at town meetings in Vermont and elsewhere increasingly use the forum to debate polarizing national and international events.
item.content:encoded:
Typically concerned with local issues, residents at town meetings in Vermont and elsewhere increasingly use the forum to debate polarizing national and international events.

(Image credit: JOSEPH PREZIOSO)
item.dc:creator: Tovia Smith

item.title: Supreme Court blocks redrawing of New York congressional map, dealing a win for GOP
item.description: At issue is the mid-term redrawing of New York's 11th Congressional District, including Staten Island and a small part of Brooklyn.
item.content:encoded:
At issue is the mid-term redrawing of New York's 11th Congressional District, including Staten Island and a small part of Brooklyn.

(Image credit: Win McNamee)
item.dc:creator: Zoe Sobel

item.title: U.S. states take steps to guard against any potential threat from Iran
item.description: Iran has made prior attempts to launch terrorist attacks on U.S. soil, but all have been thwarted in recent years. States are bracing for a heightened threat after the war.
item.content:encoded: Iran has made prior attempts to launch terrorist attacks on U.S. soil, but all have been thwarted in recent years. States are bracing for a heightened threat after the war.
item.dc:creator: Ryan Lucas

item.title: Video of Clinton depositions in Epstein investigation released by House Republicans
item.description: Over hours of testimony, the Clintons both denied knowledge of Epstein's crimes prior to his pleading guilty in 2008 to state charges in Florida for soliciting prostitution from an underage girl.
item.content:encoded:
Over hours of testimony, the Clintons both denied knowledge of Epstein's crimes prior to his pleading guilty in 2008 to state charges in Florida for soliciting prostitution from an underage girl.

(Image credit: Melina Mara)
item.dc:creator: Jason Breslow

item.title: Some Middle East flights resume, but thousands of travelers are still stranded by war
item.description: Limited flights out of the Middle East resumed on Monday. But hundreds of thousands of travelers are still stranded in the region after attacks on Iran by the U.S. and Israel.
item.content:encoded:
Limited flights out of the Middle East resumed on Monday. But hundreds of thousands of travelers are still stranded in the region after attacks on Iran by the U.S. and Israel.

(Image credit: Fadel Senna)
item.dc:creator: Joel Rose

item.title: 'Hamnet' star Jessie Buckley looks for the 'shadowy bits' of her characters
item.description: Buckley has been nominated for a best actress Oscar for her portrayal of William Shakespeare's wife in Hamnet. The film "brought me into this next chapter of my life as a mother," Buckley says.
item.content:encoded:
Hamnet.'/>

Buckley has been nominated for a best actress Oscar for her portrayal of William Shakespeare's wife in Hamnet. The film "brought me into this next chapter of my life as a mother," Buckley says.

(Image credit: Kate Green)
item.dc:creator: Terry Gross

item.title: How, who, and why: NPR flips its famous letters to defend the right to be curious
item.description: NPR is standing up for the public's right to ask hard questions in a national campaign dubbed "For your right to be curious." At NPR's headquarters, on billboards in New York City, Chicago, and Washington, D.C., and across social media, NPR's three iconic letters transform into "how," "who," and "why" — a bold declaration of its commitment to fight for Americans' right to ask questions both big and small.
item.content:encoded:
NPR is standing up for the public's right to ask hard questions in a national campaign dubbed "For your right to be curious." At NPR's headquarters, on billboards in New York City, Chicago, and Washington, D.C., and across social media, NPR's three iconic letters transform into "how," "who," and "why" — a bold declaration of its commitment to fight for Americans' right to ask questions both big and small.

(Image credit: Ryan Collerd)

item.title: Oil prices surge, but no panic yet, as Iran war continues
item.description: Global oil prices are in the high $70s as traffic through Strait of Hormuz comes to a halt. Some analysts have warned they could top $100 a barrel if the stoppage is prolonged.
item.content:encoded:
Global oil prices are in the high $70s as traffic through Strait of Hormuz comes to a halt. Some analysts have warned they could top $100 a barrel if the stoppage is prolonged.

(Image credit: Fadel Senna)
item.dc:creator: Camila Domonoske

item.title: Trump defends Iran strikes, offers objectives for military operation
item.description: His remarks are the first public ones to reporters since the U.S.-Israeli military operation against Iran began Saturday despite weeks of talks designed to stave off a conflict.
item.content:encoded:
His remarks are the first public ones to reporters since the U.S.-Israeli military operation against Iran began Saturday despite weeks of talks designed to stave off a conflict.

(Image credit: Andrew Harnik)
item.dc:creator: NPR Washington Desk

item.title: Ivermectin is making a post-pandemic comeback, among cancer patients
item.description: The anti-parasitic drug became a household name during the COVID-19 pandemic, and it is now being embraced as an alternative treatment for cancer. It is as politically polarizing as ever.
item.content:encoded: The anti-parasitic drug became a household name during the COVID-19 pandemic, and it is now being embraced as an alternative treatment for cancer. It is as politically polarizing as ever.
item.dc:creator: Yuki Noguchi
